MAT Capstone · Summative Portfolio

Kimberly Davis-Stepney

Welcome to my Professional Portfolio. This portfolio represents my journey through the Master of Arts in Teaching program with a concentration in Family and Consumer Sciences at Valdosta State University. Throughout this program, I have developed knowledge, skills, and professional dispositions necessary to become an effective educator. This website highlights my coursework, teaching philosophy, professional growth, and educational experiences. It serves as a reflection of my commitment to lifelong learning and my passion for preparing students with practical life skills that support success in school, careers, and everyday life.

Recipe for an Educator
SERVES: A full classroom · PREP TIME: One master's degree

Ingredients

  • 1 heaping cup of high expectations
  • 2 tbsp encouragement, packed
  • A generous pinch of structure and grace
  • Real-world experience, folded in gently
  • Curiosity about emerging technology, to taste

Method

Combine high expectations with encouragement until a learning environment forms where students feel respected, challenged, and empowered to reach their full potential. Fold in real-world experience so academic and life skills develop side by side.

Yield: Confident, career-ready students — and one lifelong learner who keeps growing alongside them.
01 · About

About Me

Hello, and welcome to my portfolio! My name is Kimberly Davis-Stepney, and I am a passionate educator dedicated to preparing students for success both in and beyond the classroom. I currently teach Culinary Arts in a College and Career Academy, where I have the opportunity to help students develop technical skills, workplace readiness, confidence, and a lifelong appreciation for learning.

My educational journey has been driven by a commitment to continuous growth and service. I earned a Bachelor of Science degree in Paralegal Studies and am currently completing my Master of Arts in Teaching with a concentration in Family and Consumer Sciences. Through my graduate studies, I have expanded my knowledge of instructional strategies, classroom management, technology integration, assessment practices, and student-centered learning. These experiences have strengthened my belief that every student can succeed when provided with meaningful opportunities, clear expectations, and supportive relationships.

As an educator, I believe that structure and grace go hand in hand. I strive to create a learning environment where students feel respected, challenged, and empowered to reach their full potential. My teaching philosophy combines high expectations with encouragement, helping students develop both academic and life skills that will serve them well in their future careers and personal lives.

Outside of the classroom, I enjoy spending time with my family, exploring new recipes, and finding creative ways to connect real-world experiences to learning. I am passionate about culinary arts, career and technical education, and helping students discover their strengths and interests. I also enjoy learning about emerging technologies and exploring how they can be used responsibly and effectively in education.

This portfolio represents my growth throughout the Master of Arts in Teaching program and highlights the knowledge, skills, and experiences that have shaped me as an educator. I am proud of the work showcased here and look forward to continuing my journey as a lifelong learner and Family and Consumer Sciences professional.

02 · Reflection

Critical Philosophy of Education

Education is more than a transmission of knowledge; it is the foundation for developing informed, responsible, and compassionate young people who are prepared to contribute to society. As a Family and Consumer Sciences teacher specializing in Culinary Arts, I believe schools should provide students with both academic knowledge and the practical life skills necessary to succeed in college, careers, and their communities. My philosophy of education is grounded primarily in essentialism, incorporating elements of constructivism and social reconstructionism. Together, these orientations shape my beliefs about the purpose of education, the role of the teacher, and the responsibility of schools to prepare students for lifelong learning.

Essentialism serves as the foundation of my educational philosophy because it emphasizes mastery of core knowledge, high expectations, discipline, and personal responsibility. William C. Bagley (1938) argued that schools should provide students with the essential knowledge and skills necessary for productive citizenship and lifelong success, and that systematic, sequenced instruction under a knowledgeable teacher — not incidental or unguided discovery — is what builds genuine competence. As a culinary arts instructor, I believe students must first master foundational concepts such as food safety, sanitation, knife skills, culinary terminology, and professional kitchen procedures before advancing to more complex techniques. Establishing clear expectations, consistent routines, and accountability — principles central to Bagley's (1938) platform — creates a structured learning environment where students can develop confidence, competence, and professionalism.

While I value a structured learning environment, I also believe students learn best through active participation and meaningful experiences. This belief aligns with constructivism and the work of John Dewey (1938), who argued that education should be rooted in experience and problem-solving, and that not all experience is automatically educative; experience should be organized and connected to prior learning to produce real growth. Culinary education naturally provides opportunities for experiential learning as students apply mathematical reasoning through recipe conversions, scientific principles through food chemistry, literacy skills through recipe interpretation, and collaboration through kitchen laboratory experiences. Rather than simply memorizing information, students develop deeper understanding by applying concepts in authentic situations that mirror the expectations of the culinary industry — a constructivist emphasis that Dewey (1938) placed at the center of meaningful schooling.

My philosophy is also influenced by social reconstructionism, which frames education as a means of improving society and preparing students to become engaged citizens. George Counts (1932) challenged teachers to use schools as a deliberate force for shaping a more just social order rather than simply transmitting the existing one, insisting that educators could not evade responsibility for the kind of society their students would go on to build. Food is deeply connected to culture, community, and human relationships. In my classroom, students explore diverse cuisines, examine cultural traditions, and develop an appreciation for perspectives different from their own. Through service-learning opportunities, career exploration, and community partnerships, students learn that culinary skills extend beyond the kitchen and can positively impact individuals and communities, consistent with Counts's (1932) vision of schooling as a force for social improvement. I believe education should empower students to think critically, solve problems ethically, and contribute meaningfully to the world around them.

These philosophical beliefs directly influence my instructional practices. I view my role as both educator and mentor who creates a safe, respectful, and inclusive classroom environment where students have the opportunity to succeed. I believe students thrive when expectations are clear, instruction is engaging, and mistakes are treated as opportunities for growth rather than failure — an approach that draws on Bagley's (1938) insistence on discipline and Dewey's (1938) insistence on learning through experience in equal measure. Building trusting relationships with students, families, colleagues, and community partners creates a supportive learning environment that encourages confidence, resilience, and personal responsibility.

Technology also plays an important role in my philosophy of education. I intentionally integrate digital tools to enhance instruction, differentiate learning, and promote collaboration. These resources provide multiple pathways for students to access content, demonstrate understanding, and develop digital literacy skills essential for success in today's workforce. However, technology should complement, not replace, the authentic hands-on learning experiences that are central to culinary arts education.

Ultimately, I believe the purpose of education is to prepare students not only for employment but also for lifelong learning and responsible citizenship. Success is measured by more than academic achievement; it is reflected in students' ability to think critically, communicate effectively, collaborate with others, and adapt to new challenges. My goal is to inspire students to become confident, ethical, and resilient individuals who continue learning long after they leave my classroom. Every lesson I teach is an opportunity to develop both technical competence and strong character, empowering students to build meaningful futures while making positive contributions to their communities.

References

Bagley, W. C. (1938). An essentialist's platform for the advancement of American education. Educational Administration and Supervision, 24(4), 241–256.

Counts, G. S. (1932). Dare the school build a new social order? John Day Company.

Dewey, J. (1938). Experience and education. Macmillan.

06 · Technology

Gallery of Links & Technology Integration

Teacher-Approved Websites and Apps

The following digital tools and websites are regularly used in my Culinary Arts classroom to enhance instruction, support student learning, encourage collaboration, and prepare students with the technology skills needed for college and career success.

Website / AppHow It Supports Student Learning
Canvas LMSServes as the learning management system where students access assignments, instructional materials, announcements, grades, and feedback. instructure.com/canvas
Canva for EducationAllows students to design menus, recipe cards, infographics, presentations, and other creative culinary projects. canva.com/education
WaygroundProvides engaging formative assessments, interactive review games, and immediate feedback to monitor student understanding. wayground.com
NearpodDelivers interactive lessons that include polls, quizzes, videos, and collaborative activities to increase student engagement. nearpod.com
Google DocsSupports collaborative writing, recipe analysis, lab reflections, and note-taking. workspace.google.com/docs
Google SlidesEnables students to create presentations showcasing culinary techniques, career research, and nutrition topics. workspace.google.com/slides
Google KeepHelps students organize notes, create checklists for labs, and manage assignments and projects. keep.google.com
ServSafeProvides industry-recognized food safety training and certification resources that prepare students for careers in the foodservice industry. servsafe.com
TeachShareProvides instructional resources, lesson ideas, and standards-aligned teaching materials to support classroom instruction. teachshare.com
SafeShare TVAllows students to view instructional culinary videos in a distraction-free environment by removing advertisements and unnecessary content. safeshare.tv
MagicSchool AISupports teacher planning by generating differentiated instructional materials, rubrics, assessments, accommodations, and classroom resources that meet diverse student learning needs. magicschool.ai

Technology Integration

Teacher Use of Technology to Customize Instruction

As a Culinary Arts instructor, I use TeachShare and Google Slides to design and deliver standards-based instruction that meets the diverse needs of my students. TeachShare provides access to instructional resources, lesson plans, and activities that align with Georgia CTAE standards. These resources allow me to adapt lessons, incorporate best practices, and provide engaging learning experiences for students with varying readiness levels.

I use Google Slides to create visually engaging presentations that include photographs, diagrams, vocabulary, step-by-step cooking procedures, and embedded instructional videos. Students benefit from having access to these presentations through Canvas, allowing them to review concepts before, during, and after laboratory activities. This approach supports visual learners, English language learners, and students who need additional time or repeated instruction while also providing enrichment opportunities for advanced learners.

Student Use of Technology to Enhance Learning

Students use technology to demonstrate their understanding, collaborate with classmates, and create authentic culinary products. Throughout the course, students use Canva to design professional-quality menus, recipe cards, nutrition infographics, food safety posters, and career exploration projects that strengthen both their culinary knowledge and digital design skills. Students also use Google Slides to develop presentations that explain cooking techniques, kitchen safety procedures, culinary careers, and laboratory reflections. These presentations encourage students to communicate their learning while incorporating images, research, and multimedia elements.

To stay organized during culinary laboratories and long-term projects, students use Google Keep to create digital checklists, record recipe notes, manage assignments, and track project deadlines. This tool promotes organization, responsibility, and independent learning while helping students develop workplace skills that transfer beyond the classroom.

Integrating technology into my Culinary Arts classroom has transformed the way students learn and demonstrate their understanding. Digital tools provide opportunities for collaboration, creativity, differentiation, and authentic assessment while supporting hands-on laboratory experiences. By combining industry-recognized resources with engaging instructional technology, I help students develop the technical, digital, and employability skills necessary for success in college, careers, and lifelong learning.
